title: Scottish tablet
subtitle: Hard, grainy Scottish fudge made with condensed milk and granulated sugar
excerpt: Scottish tablet is made by boiling condensed milk, granulated sugar, butter, and milk together to soft-ball stage, then beating the mixture as it cools to create a grainy, slightly crystalline texture. It is firmer and crumblier than English fudge and does not have fudge's smooth, yielding texture. The grain comes from beating: stopping too late gives toffee, too early gives fudge.
sourceNotes: Scottish tablet has been documented in Scottish recipe books from at least the 18th century. Mrs McLintock, Receipts for Cookery and Pastry-Work (1736), one of the earliest Scottish cookbooks, includes a recipe for tablet made with sugar and rosewater. The modern version with condensed milk became standard after condensed milk was widely available in Scotland from the late 19th century. Tablet is associated with Highland Games, school fairs, and Christmas in Scotland. The distinctive grain (firmer and more crystalline than English fudge) comes from the beating stage performed while the mixture is still warm.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze wrapped tablet in an airtight container for up to 3 months. Defrost at room temperature.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Tablet keeps well in an airtight tin at room temperature for up to 3 weeks. Separate layers with baking paper.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: target sugar temperature. Soft-ball stage: 113 to 118°C on a sugar thermometer. A drop of the mixture in cold water should form a soft, pliable ball. This temperature is the point at which the mixture is removed from the heat and beaten.

recipeTools.notes: heavy-based, deep saucepan — the mixture bubbles up significantly
lightly buttered, or use a 20 cm square tin

glossaryTerms.definition: The characteristic texture of Scottish tablet: slightly crystalline, grainy, and firm, distinct from the smooth, yielding texture of English fudge. The grain is created by beating the cooked sugar mixture as it cools. Beating encourages small sugar crystals to form throughout the mass. Stopping too early (while the mixture is too warm) gives a soft, fudge-like result; stopping too late (when the mixture is cold) gives a solid block that cannot be poured. The correct time to stop is when the mixture thickens and begins to lose its gloss but is still pourable.

content.text: Scottish tablet is distinguished from fudge by its

. The beating stage is where tablet goes right or wrong. Begin beating as soon as the pan is off the heat. Beat with a wooden spoon for 5 to 10 minutes; the mixture will thicken and start to look dull. Pour into the tin at this point; not before (too runny, fudge texture) and not after (sets in the pan, impossible to pour).

Butter a 30 × 20 cm tin or 20 cm square tin. Put the sugar, condensed milk, butter, and milk in a large, deep, heavy-based saucepan over low heat. Stir until the sugar has dissolved completely. Increase the heat to medium and bring to a steady boil, stirring frequently to prevent the bottom catching. Cook until the mixture reaches 116°C on a sugar thermometer (soft-ball stage), about 25 to 30 minutes from reaching the boil.

Remove from the heat immediately. Stir in the vanilla. Beat the mixture with a wooden spoon for 5 to 10 minutes, working continuously. The tablet will begin to thicken and lose its gloss. When it looks dull and has noticeably thickened, pour immediately into the prepared tin. Tilt the tin to level. Leave to cool and set completely at room temperature, about 2 hours. Mark into squares with a sharp knife while still slightly warm, then leave to set fully before breaking apart.
